w:r.w:t: AstraFlow is a modular, developer-first ETL and pipeline orchestration platform that enables structured and semi-structured data movement across heterogeneous sources. Engineered for control, extensibility, and scale — AstraFlow competes in the space of Airbyte, Fivetran, and lightweight Databricks — but with cleaner architecture and a visual DAG-based pipeline builder at its core.
Enterprises and growing data teams face a fragmented landscape of ETL tools:
Airbyte is powerful but operationally heavy and hard to self-host cleanly.
Fivetran is expensive and limits control over transformation logic.
Databricks is overkill for mid-size pipelines and has steep onboarding cost.
Custom scripts are not maintainable, have no observability, and break silently.

AstraFlow fills the gap: a clean, self-hostable ETL platform with visual pipeline building, execution monitoring, and a modular connector architecture — usable today as an internal tool, scalable tomorrow as a SaaS product.
ETL & Data Integration Tools — $15B+ and growing at 14% CAGR
No clean, developer-friendly, self-hostable visual ETL for mid-market
Clean architecture, extensible connectors, DAG-first design
Build a working MVP that can run real data pipelines end-to-end.
Demonstrate AstraFlow in internal/internship environments (DS Group context).
Position the product as a portfolio cornerstone and startup foundation.
Lay technical groundwork for multi-tenant SaaS commercialization.
Enable users to configure, build, and run ETL pipelines visually.
Support at least 7 connection types: MySQL, PostgreSQL, Snowflake, MSSQL, MongoDB, Oracle, CSV/JSON.
Provide real-time execution monitoring and node-level logging.
Allow pipeline scheduling via cron expressions.
Deliver a clean, modern UI with dashboard-level system visibility.
Apache Spark / big data engine integration — Phase 5.
SaaS connector marketplace (Stripe, Shopify, etc.) — Phase 6.
Builds and manages pipelines, cares about reliability and debuggability.
Core power user — needs full control over nodes, transforms, execution logs.
Creates data models and transformation logic, uses SQL heavily.
Needs SQL transform nodes, schema preview, and column mapping.
Consumes pipeline output for reporting and visualization.
Needs scheduled pipelines and data catalog to discover available datasets.
Manages connection credentials and system configurations.
Needs secure credential storage, connection testing, and system settings.
AstraFlow is organized into 7 core modules. Each module contains a set of features with priority levels (P0 = must have MVP, P1 = important, P2 = nice-to-have).
The command center — provides a real-time system overview for all pipeline activity.
The most critical foundational module. Manages all source and destination data connections with secure credential storage.
Browse tables and columns for a connection
Standard relational DB — most common in FMCG/enterprise environments
Default analytics DB — used widely in modern data stacks
Cloud data warehouse — critical for DS Group integration context
Legacy enterprise DB — required for FMCG sector clients
Flat file ingestion — most common ad-hoc data source
Visual node-based pipeline editor. Supports drag-and-drop composition of Source → Transform → Destination flows.
Source Nodes: DB Table, Query, File Upload
Transform Nodes: Filter, Select Columns, Rename, Basic SQL
Destination Nodes: DB Table Write, File Export
The backend orchestration backbone — runs pipelines step by step with retry and queue management.
Full observability into pipeline execution with trace-level logging.
Dataset discovery and schema documentation — the feature that differentiates AstraFlow from pure ETL tools.
Automated pipeline execution triggered by cron schedules with basic failure alerting.
As a Data Engineer, I want to add a Snowflake connection so I can pull data from our cloud warehouse.
As a Data Engineer, I want to test a connection before saving it so I know my credentials are correct.
As an Analytics Engineer, I want to build a pipeline visually from a MySQL source to a PostgreSQL destination.
As a Data Engineer, I want to add a filter transform node to exclude rows with null values.
As a Platform Admin, I want to run a pipeline manually and see each step execute in sequence.
As a Data Engineer, I want to see node-level logs when a pipeline fails so I can debug quickly.
As an Analytics Engineer, I want to schedule a pipeline to run every night at 2am.
As a Data Analyst, I want to browse available datasets and preview their schema.
As a Data Engineer, I want to retry a failed job without re-running the whole pipeline.
As a Platform Admin, I want all credentials stored encrypted, never in plaintext.
< 2s from trigger to first node execution
Failed jobs must retry automatically up to 3 times (configurable).
Execution queue must survive Node.js worker crashes (Redis-backed).
Partial pipeline failures must not corrupt destination data.
All connection credentials stored AES-256 encrypted.
No plaintext secrets in logs, APIs, or database.
API endpoints protected with JWT-based authentication.
Architecture supports horizontal worker scaling via multiple BullMQ consumers.
PostgreSQL schema designed to support multi-tenant upgrade in Phase 4.
Python worker layer ready to plug in for computationally heavy transforms.
> 95% of triggered pipelines complete without fatal error
Engineer can isolate pipeline failure in < 5 min via logs
New connection configured, tested, and saved in < 3 min
Basic ETL pipeline (source → filter → destination) built in < 10 min
Cron-triggered pipelines fire within ±60 seconds of schedule
Canvas DAG editor, node types, save/load pipelines
BullMQ workers, step execution, retry logic
Execution history, node logs, status tracking
Schema viewer, dataset listing, table preview
Auto pipeline generation, schema mapping AI
Single-user deployment for MVP — no team collaboration or RBAC needed.
Self-hosted on Linux server (Ubuntu 22+) — not cloud-managed initially.
Internet connectivity available for npm packages and Node.js runtime.
Data volumes handled in MVP are moderate (< 10M rows per pipeline run).
React Flow implementation may have edge cases with complex graphs.
Start with linear pipelines; add branching post-MVP.
Connecting to Oracle/MSSQL may require proprietary drivers.
Use community Node.js drivers; document known limitations.
Redis crashes can lose pending jobs.
Use Redis persistence (AOF mode); store job metadata in PostgreSQL.
Mishandling secrets is a critical risk.
AES-256 + env-based key management from Day 1.
Adding AI or SaaS features too early kills focus.
Strict phase gating — no Phase N+1 work before Phase N is stable.
